# Medical Education

Research and news on artificial intelligence in medical education.

Items are selected nightly by an automated pipeline and link to their original sources. See [About](../about.md) for how selection works.

<img class="section-banner" src="../../assets/section-medical-education.svg" alt="">

<div class="section-brief">
<p>Research highlighting significant sex and racial biases in physician images generated by text-to-image artificial intelligence underscores ongoing concerns about representation in medical education tools <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42376226?fc=20260609215449&amp;ff=20260630092944&amp;v=2.20.0">[1]</a>. A rapid scoping review details broad integration of artificial intelligence across U.S. healthcare professional degree programs, demonstrating increasing institutional adoption <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42370260?fc=20260609215449&amp;ff=20260629131807&amp;v=2.20.0">[6]</a>. Meanwhile, studies continue to expose gaps in medical student readiness for artificial intelligence, with findings that Korean medical students lack sufficient preparation to use these technologies effectively in training <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42376791?fc=20260609215449&amp;ff=20260630132631&amp;v=2.20.0">[4]</a>. In clinical pathology education, the reliability of large language models in generating multiple-choice questions is under active evaluation <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42376175?fc=20260609215449&amp;ff=20260630092944&amp;v=2.20.0">[2]</a>, reflecting both potential and limitations for automated assessment content.</p>
<p class="section-brief-date">The picture as of June 30, 2026; numbered links go to the items below.</p>
</div>
